bins.name: Untested retail jurisdiction claim
bins.desc: Consumers Energy argues that asserting federal jurisdiction over large load interconnection as open-access transmission service is a departure from historical practice and risks encroaching on the states' authority over retail electric sales. The filer asks FERC to confirm that any jurisdictional action will not alter the retail classification of sales to large load customers or create undue preference.
quotes: interconnection of retail load has not, in the past, been considered to be a 'critical component of open access transmission service.' If the Commission decides to assert such jurisdiction, it would be an untested departure from historical practice.

the Commission should clarify that any potential assertion of Commission jurisdiction over the interconnection of large load customers to the transmission system will not change the retail nature of sales to such customers or result in undue preference to them.

not prejudging the best approach to advancing goals set forth in the ANOPR may mean that the Commission determines that a sweeping federal rule is not the best path forward, particularly if it may frustrate state initiatives that are currently in place or being developed.

bins.name: Raise threshold to 100 MW
bins.desc: The filer contends the proposed 20 MW threshold is far too low and would capture ordinary retail customers unrelated to data centers. Consumers Energy recommends a 100 MW nationwide threshold to keep any reforms narrowly targeted to the problem the ANOPR seeks to address.
quotes: a 20 MW threshold for load interconnections would capture a myriad of customers that come nowhere close to the data centers and other major industrial loads driving the ANOPR's proposed reforms.
Consumers Energy submits that a 100 MW nationwide threshold would be more appropriate than the ANOPR's 20 MW proposal.

bins.name: Joint study could slow generation
bins.desc: Consumers Energy is concerned that changes to study processes for large loads and hybrid facilities could slow the interconnection of new generation, which the Administration has made a priority. The filer urges the Commission to evaluate those impacts carefully before directing any changes.
quotes: the Commission should carefully evaluate how such changes could impact and potentially slow the interconnection of new generation that the Administration has made a priority.

bins.name: Option to build not appropriate for load
bins.desc: The filer argues that large load customers are not analogous to generation owners and should not receive an equivalent option to build interconnection facilities. Extending that right to data centers and industrial customers without grid expertise creates reliability risk.
quotes: large load customers should not be entitled to the same option to build as generation owners.

bins.name: Reliability risk from load-side build option
bins.desc: Consumers Energy warns that large load customers lack the industry expertise generators have accumulated over decades, making an option to build interconnection facilities a reliability hazard. The Commission lacks sufficient record to support extending that option.
quotes: the Company is concerned that allowing large load customers the option to build may carry unintended reliability risks given that many large load customers are data centers or other large industrial customers that lack the industry expertise required to ensure safe and reliable grid operations.

bins.name: Expedited track burdens RTO study resources
bins.desc: The filer argues that an expedited study track for curtailable large loads and hybrids will draw on the same RTO/ISO resources needed for generation interconnection studies, potentially compounding delays rather than alleviating them.
quotes: Certain ANOPR proposals, such as the notion that 'the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable should be expedited,' will burden RTO/ISO resources that are also working to perform timely studies of new generation.

bins.name: Study changes risk generation delays
bins.desc: Consumers Energy cautions that several ANOPR study proposals could slow generation interconnection timelines at a moment when the Administration has declared new dispatchable generation a priority. The filer urges targeted, careful changes and points to MISO's existing ERAS expedited process as evidence that the region is already under study-queue pressure.
quotes: the Company is concerned that allowing large load customers the option to build may carry unintended reliability risks given that many large load customers are data centers or other large industrial customers that lack the industry expertise required to ensure safe and reliable grid operations.
large load customers should not be entitled to the same option to build as generation owners.
the Commission should carefully evaluate how such changes could impact and potentially slow the interconnection of new generation that the Administration has made a priority.

Certain ANOPR proposals, such as the notion that 'the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable should be expedited,' will burden RTO/ISO resources that are also working to perform timely studies of new generation.
RTOs, ISOs, transmission owners, and generation owners have all worked diligently to incorporate the reforms included in Order No. 2023 and need regulatory certainty moving forward.

bins.name: MISO queue capacity at risk
bins.desc: As a MISO member, Consumers Energy notes that MISO has already implemented reforms such as the ERAS process to speed generation interconnection. Adding expedited large load study tracks would burden MISO's limited study resources and could undercut those improvements.
quotes: Certain ANOPR proposals, such as the notion that 'the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable should be expedited,' will burden RTO/ISO resources that are also working to perform timely studies of new generation.

bins.name: Rulemaking timeline too compressed
bins.desc: Consumers Energy argues that finalizing a rule of this magnitude in roughly six months risks a poorly reasoned outcome and legal challenges. The filer compares the proceeding to Order Nos. 1000 and 2023, each of which took more than 13 months, and suggests a sweeping federal rule may not even be the right instrument.
quotes: attempting to finalize a rulemaking of this potential magnitude within approximately six months may compromise the end result and increase the risk of legal challenges concerning the Commission's authority and the rule's legality.

not prejudging the best approach to advancing goals set forth in the ANOPR may mean that the Commission determines that a sweeping federal rule is not the best path forward, particularly if it may frustrate state initiatives that are currently in place or being developed.

bins.name: Stakeholders need post-2023 stability
bins.desc: The filer notes that RTOs, ISOs, transmission owners, and generators have invested heavily in implementing Order No. 2023 reforms and need regulatory certainty before facing another wave of interconnection process changes.
quotes: RTOs, ISOs, transmission owners, and generation owners have all worked diligently to incorporate the reforms included in Order No. 2023 and need regulatory certainty moving forward.
